coredata.dc:title: Spectroscopic investigation on the binding interactions between graphene quantum dots and carbonic anhydrase
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaojuan
Zhang, Zhixiong
Liu, Zhenzhen
Ma, Xiqi
Dai, Qi
Wang, Xiaoqiang
Ge, Baosheng
He, Hua
Huang, Fang
coredata.dc:description: As a new member of the nanomaterials family, ultrasmall graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown broad application prospects in the field of biomedicine, but the analysis of their biological effects at the molecular level is yet limited. Herein, carbonic anhydrase (CA) was selected as a model protein to assess the interactions between GQDs and biomacromolecules. A range of spectroscopic techniques were employed to systematically investigate the binding interactions between GQDs and CA and the catalytic function of CA in the presence of GQDs was evaluated. Experimental results showed that GQDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of CA and the concentration dependent quenching efficiency exhibited an obvious deviation from the linear plot, indicating a static binding mode. Further investigation suggested that van der Waal interactions and hydrogen bonding were the main driving forces. Additionally, circular dichroism measurement showed that the binding of GQDs induced slight conformational changes of CA. The catalytic capability assessment proved that these binding interactions resulted in the reduction of the biological functions of CA. This comprehensive study provided important insight into the interaction of GQDs with biomacromolecules, which would be crucial for the further applications of GQDs and other nanomaterials in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Carbonic anhydrase
Fluorescence
Binding interaction

Conformational change of carbonic anhydrase induced by GQDs
Catalytic performance change of carbonic anhydrase induced by GQDs

ZR2019ZD17), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (18CX02126A), and the Graduate Innovative Engineering Funding project of UPC (YCX2020041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of GQDs (the inset shows a photograph of GQDs solution of 0.5 mg mL−1 under a 365 nm UV lamp).
(A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of GQDs. (B) Histogram depicting the size distribution of GQDs.

The fluorescence spectra of 0.67 μM CA in the presence of different concentrations of GQDs. The inset corresponds to the Stern-Volmer plot of FL intensity at 345 nm. (A), (B), and (C) are experimental data collected at 298, 304 and 310 K, respectively. The concentrations of GQDs are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 μM, respectively.
Stern-Volmer plots (A), modified Stern-Volmer plots (B), and van’t Hoff plot (C) of GQDs–CA system. The concentration of CA was 0.67 μM while the CGQDs were 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 μM, respectively.
CD spectra of CA and GQDs–CA system. The concentration of CA was 13.4 μM. The concentrations of GQDs were 5 and 10 μM, respectively.
Relative activity of 0.1 μM CA in the presence of different concentrations of GQDs.
Stern–Volmer quenching constants K SV, associative binding constants K a and relative thermodynamic parameters.
β-Strand contents of CA and GQDs-CA systems.

head.ce:title: Spectroscopic investigation on the binding interactions between graphene quantum dots and carbonic anhydrase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new member of the nanomaterials family, ultrasmall graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown broad application prospects in the field of biomedicine, but the analysis of their biological effects at the molecular level is yet limited. Herein, carbonic anhydrase (CA) was selected as a model protein to assess the interactions between GQDs and biomacromolecules. A range of spectroscopic techniques were employed to systematically investigate the binding interactions between GQDs and CA and the catalytic function of CA in the presence of GQDs was evaluated. Experimental results showed that GQDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of CA and the concentration dependent quenching efficiency exhibited an obvious deviation from the linear plot, indicating a static binding mode. Further investigation suggested that van der Waal interactions and hydrogen bonding were the main driving forces. Additionally, circular dichroism measurement showed that the binding of GQDs induced slight conformational changes of CA. The catalytic capability assessment proved that these binding interactions resulted in the reduction of the biological functions of CA. This comprehensive study provided important insight into the interaction of GQDs with biomacromolecules, which would be crucial for the further applications of GQDs and other nanomaterials in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene materials have received great attention from scientific researchers since their discovery. In recent years, scientists have further developed zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Benefiting from the remarkable fluorescence characteristics, good aqueous dispersion, and excellent biocompatibility [1], GQDs have shown very attractive application prospects in biochemical and biomedical fields such as cellular imaging [2], drug delivery [3], biosensing [4], and antibacterial [5].

With the rapid development of functionalized GQDs, the importance of systematic evaluation of their biological effects has become prominent. Lee et al. [6,7] expanded the in vivo research on the biodistribution, serum biochemistry, and complete blood count study of GQDs, which revealed that GQDs did not cause appreciable toxicity in the level of animal body and organ. It needs to be noted that when GQDs are used for living systems, they interact inevitably with various bioactive molecules, which is the vital process affecting their following behavior including the circulation time, cellular internalization, and targeting efficiency [8–10]. Whether the presence of GQDs would affect the conformation and function of those biomolecules? A comprehensive study on this question is crucial for the practical application of GQDs.

Research in this direction has been carried out with the well-known semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [11–18]. However, as a new member of nanomaterials family GQDs show some significant differences. Firstly, QDs often contain metal ions (such as Cd2+) but GQDs normally consist of non-metallic elements only, which endows them better biocompatibility and application potential. Secondly, QDs are spherical nanoparticles ranging from 3 to 8 nm, while GQDs are planar nanocrystals of graphene with < 0.8 nm thickness and 1–4 nm diameter [19,20]. The specific conformation of GQDs as well as their surfaces with abundant aromatic rings and edges rich in carboxyl / hydroxyl groups lead to the interactions with biomacromolecules distinguished from conventional QDs. Proteins are important components in organisms and their functions are closely related to the specific three-dimensional conformations. Due to the ultrasmall size and the unique morphology, GQDs might attach onto the protein surfaces or combine with some domains or residues. Therefore, the investigation on the interactions between GQDs and proteins will help us understand the potential toxicity of GQDs at the molecular level. Researchers have made preliminary explorations on the interactions between graphene-based materials and various protein molecules [21–23]. However, the research objects mainly focus on the large-sized (micron-level) two-dimensional graphene materials. The analysis of the biological effects of GQDs is yet relatively limited. Xiao and coworkers reported the pioneering investigation on the molecular interaction of GQDs with human serum albumin (HSA) and the results showed that GQDs form complex with HSA, leading to the conformational change of HSA [6]. This work further verified the importance of a systematic study on the interactions between GQDs and protein molecules. Herein, we choose carbonic anhydrase (CA) as a model protein to investigate its interaction with GQDs.

CA has been employed as a model protein in a variety of studies in biophysics, bioanalysis, and medicinal chemistry due to its excellent stability under standard laboratory conditions and its well-understood catalytic activity [24–27]. In vivo, this enzyme is a class of proteases ubiquitous, which catalyzes the interconversion of
CO 2
and bicarbonate [28]. These enzymes are involved in some vital physiological processes associated with the acid-base balance regulating and the carbon dioxide discharging [29]. Moreover, CA is the target for many drugs, such as acetazolamide and dichlorphenamide [30]. Whether or not the function of CA can be stably exercised is an important assessment item for determining the toxicity of GQDs at the molecular level.

Spectroscopic measurement is a classic and effective method to study the molecular interactions. In the past two decades, this method has been used to investigate the interactions between proteins and various drug molecules [31–33] and nanomaterials [34–37]. In this article, a range of spectroscopic techniques, including UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission, as well as circular dichroism, were employed to analyze the molecular interactions between GQDs and CA, and the thermodynamic parameters and the driving force of the binding interactions were determined. Moreover, the conformational change of CA induced by GQDs and the effect of interactions on CA function were also investigated. Our work may provide valuable information to assess the potential toxicity risk of GQDs at the molecular level.

GQDs were synthesized as previously described [38,39]. Briefly, carbon black (0.2 g) was refluxed with nitric acid (50 mL, 6 M) for 24 h. The obtained suspension was cooled to room temperature (20–25 ℃) and then centrifuged. After the pellet was discarded, and the supernatant was heated and dried. The resultant reddish-brown solid was resuspended in ultra-pure water and then filtered through a filtering membrane with a cutoff of 3 kDa. The GQDs with molecular weights (MWs) under 3 kDa were finally retained. The GQDs samples were diluted for optical characterization. The molecular weight of GQDs was expressed in terms of the molecular weight of benzene, which was about 78 g mol−1. CA was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co (China) and used as received. The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of carbonic anhydrase were shown in Fig. S1. CA was dissolved in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) and kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C. All other regents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was produced by Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and throughout the whole experiments.

UV–Vis spectrum was recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were collected using FluoroMax-4 (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100 (Japan) electron microscope at 200 kV.

All fluorescence analyses were performed on FluoroMax-4 (Horiba Jobin Yvon, JP). The spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–450 nm upon excitation at 280 nm. The widths of the excitation and emission slits were set to 4.0 nm, and each spectrum was the average of three scans. 1.0 cm quartz cells were used for all measurements. Also, respective blanks of PBS were used for the correction of all fluorescence spectra. The experiments were repeated and found to be reproducible within experimental errors. In order to correct the non-linear fluorescence response of the analyte induced by the inner filter effect, which refers to the apparent quenching of observed fluorescence due to the attenuation of excitation beam or reabsorption of emitted radiation by the chromophores. The fluorescence intensities of native CA and CA-GQDs solutions used in this study were converted by using the following equation [40],
(1)
F corrected
=
F observed
*
10
A em
+
A ex
/2

where F corrected and F observed are the corrected fluorescence intensity and the observed fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength, respectively. A em and A ex denote the absorbance at the emission and excitation wavelengths, respectively.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on MOS-450/AF-CD (Bio-Logic, France) equipped with 1.0 mm quartz cells. The CD profiles were obtained employing a scan speed of 500 nm min−1 and response time of 0.5 s. Each spectrum was the average of three successive scans and was corrected by PBS. Appropriate baseline corrections in CD spectra were made.

CA catalyzes the reversible hydration-dehydration of carbon dioxide, and H+ ions are transferred between the active site of the enzyme and the surrounding buffer [41]. Therefore, we determine the change in CA activity by measuring the concentration of hydrogen ions in the reaction system [42,43]. We measured the time that a saturated CO2 solution to lower the pH of 0.02 M Tris-HCl buffer from 8.0 to 7.0 at 0 °C to determine the activity of CA. The enzyme activity was calculated using the equation [44],
(2)
Enzyme Activity
WAU
=
T C
-
T e
T e
where Tc represents the reaction time without CA and Te represents the reaction time with CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of carbonic anhydrase by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding interactions between CA and GQDs were firstly characterized at 298 K with fluorescence spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, CA showed an obvious fluorescence emission peak centered around 340 nm when excited at 280 nm. Due to the low extinction coefficient at 280 nm, the low quantum yield, the different fluorescence emission wavelength range (peaked at 528 nm), and the low concentration (1 to 40 μM) under experimental conditions, GQDs did not exhibit obvious fluorescence in this spectroscopic recording window. Additionally, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of CA was quenched regularly when GQDs were added into the solution, and the quenching effect was strongly dependent on the concentration of GQDs, which was analyzed through the Stern–Volmer equation:
(3)
F 0
-F
F
=
K SV
[Q]

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore (CA) at 345 nm in the absence and presence of quencher (GQDs), K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher (GQDs), respectively. Inset of Fig. 3A showed that at low concentrations (<5 µM), the change of FL intensity agreed excellently with the Stern–Volmer equation whereas the results departed from the initial linearity at higher concentrations of GQDs. According to the literature, the fluorescence quenching mechanism usually includes a collisional process (dynamic quenching) and/or ground state complex formation (static quenching) between quencher and fluorophore, and the tendency of upward curvature meant that static and dynamic quenching coexisted at the higher concentrations of GQDs (>5µM) [47,48].

The fluorescence quenching of CA by GQDs was further analyzed at different temperatures. It was shown (Fig. 3B and 3C) that when the temperature was changed from 298 K to 304 and 310 K, the intrinsic fluorescence of CA did not display obvious variation and the similar upcurved deviation from the straight linear prediction was recorded. The plots {(F 0/F – 1)/[Q])}vs [Q] at various temperatures were also summarized to check the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. S2, the plots yielded a straight line parallel to x-axis when the concentration of GQDs varied from 1 to 5 μM, which changed to oblique upward straight lines when the concentrations of GQDs were higher than 5 μM. The results further proved that one type of quenching occurred at low concentrations of GQDs while two types of mechanisms coexisted at high concentrations of GQDs [49].

According to Eq. (3), the values of K SV at certain temperature can be calculated from the linear regression of the Stern-Volmer plots, which were then summarized in Fig. 4 A and Table 1 . It could be seen that under all three working temperatures, at the low concentration range of GQDs (<5 µM) a good linear relationship was obtained between the quenching effect and the quencher concentration (Fig. 4A). Additionally, the slope (the Stern–Volmer quenching constant K SV) decreased accordingly with the temperature rising, indicating that the quenching effect of GQDs at the same concentration was more effective at lower temperatures. These results suggested the static quenching mode of CA by GQDs.

With regard to the static quenching process, there is a more suitable modified Stern–Volmer equation [50] for analyzing the interactions between quenchers and fluorophores,
(4)
F 0
F 0
- F
=
F 0
Δ F
=
1
f a
K a
1
[ Q ]
+
1
f a

where f a represents the mole fraction of solvent-accessible fluorophore, and K a represents the effective quenching constant for the accessible fluorophore, which can represent the binding constant for the quencher-fluorophore system [51].

The fluorescence spectroscopic data was then analyzed according to Eq. (4) and plotted in Fig. 4B. It was shown that there was strong linear dependence between F 0/(F 0-F) and [Q]-1 (the reciprocal value of the quencher concentration). And the binding constants K a were calculated from intercept divided by slope (Table 1). Similar to the Stern–Volmer quenching constant K sv, the binding constant K a also decreased with the increasing temperature. The same trend was also reported for the static interaction analysis between protein and other nanomaterials [52,53] which suggested that under relatively high temperature, the Brownian motion is stronger, preventing the static interactions of GQDs and CA to some extent.

In order to clarify the main driving force of the binding interaction, the thermodynamic parameters were deduced from the fluorescence quenching experimental data. Hydrophobic, van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions are usually the primary binding forces between exogenous ligands and protein, which can be judged by thermodynamic parameters [54]. If the enthalpy change (ΔH) over the above temperature range is minimum, then the enthalpy change (ΔH) and the entropy change (ΔS) can be calculated from the van’t Hoff equation,
(5)
ln
K a
= -
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R

And the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) can be calculated by the following equation,
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S

In Eq. (5), K a is the binding constant at the corresponding temperature T and R is the gas constant. As shown in Fig. 4C, there was an ideal linear relationship between ln K a and 1/T. The thermodynamic parameters were summarized in Table 1, where both the negative values of ΔH and ΔS indicated that van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bond interactions played major roles during the binding of CA and GQDs [55,56]. Moreover, the binding process between GQDs and CA occurred spontaneously, which was judged by the negative values of ΔG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational change of carbonic anhydrase induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Circular dichroism spectrum (CD) is a powerful tool to investigate the conformational changes of protein molecules during the interactions with GQDs. The far UV-CD spectra of free CA (Fig. 5 ) exhibited a typical profile of a β-helix-rich secondary structure [57], while when GQDs were added to the system, the spectra showed a gradient change.

The β-strand contents were analyzed by CONTIN and the results were summarized in Table 2 . The β-strand contents of CA decreased as the concentration of GQDs increased, which indicated that the binding of GQDs caused conformational changes of CA. Huang, et al. [58] investigated the interaction between graphene oxide (GO) and trypsin. They found that the binding interaction of GO caused the secondary structure variation of trypsin with the increments of both the α-helix and the β-turn structures as well as the reductions of both the β-sheet and the β-antiparallel structures, which was partially due to the hydrogen bonds existed between the amino acid residues (threonine, lysine, serine) and the oxygen atoms of GO. It was reported that the edges of GQDs are rich in carboxyl (−COOH) and hydroxyl (−OH) groups (Fig. S3) [38,59], similar to the structure of GO. The amino acid residues of CA could also form hydrogen bonds with GQDs, leading to the variation of secondary structures of CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance change of carbonic anhydrase induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Taking into account what has been discussed above, a safe and sound conclusion could be completed that the addition of GQDs changes the structure of CA to some extent. It is known that the biological functions of proteins, including CA, are often associated with their specific structures. Therefore, it is reasonable to suspect that the catalytic function of CA would be disturbed by the binding of GQDs.

The experimental results summarized in Table S1, Fig. S4 and Fig. 6 verified this reasoning. Obvious variations in the enzyme activity of CA were measured when GQDs were added, which gradually decreased from 6.0 to 1.8 with the concentration of GQDs rising, indicating that the binding of GQDs affected the active site of CA. Tanaka, et al. [60] investigated the structure of CA and suggested that the active site of CA formed a funnel-shaped channel with a diameter of 1.3 nm, ranging from the surface of the protein to the center, and zinc ions were located at the bottom of the channel. The single-layer GQDs are 1.0 ∼ 4.0 nm in diameter with a thickness of about 0.7 nm, which means that quite a few GQDs could go through the opening of the channel to interact with the active site of CA. This type of small-size effect was also reported in a similar study on the interactions between GQDs and horseradish peroxidase [50], where it was suggested that small graphene sheets are more likely to be in complete contact with the enzyme molecules, resulting in large perturbations in the conformation of the enzyme and reducing its stability and activity. The related research on the interactions between single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) and CA [61] had shown that the non-covalent interactions, including hydrophobic, π-π stacking, and charge-transfer interactions, were responsible for the binding between SWNTs and CA. Considering the similar benzene ring structure on GQDs, those interactions very probably also existed between CA and GQDs, which led to the variation of conformation and function of CA. Moreover, the active site of CA was surrounded by β-strand to ensure the specific structure, which was also interfered by the relatively strong interaction between GQDs and β-strand, resulting in a significant decrease in catalytic performance.

The toxicity of GQDs has been considered as a possible limitation for their application in living systems and therefore, prior mechanistic toxicological experiment between GQDs and biomacromolecules is needed. Herein, the molecular interactions between GQDs and CA were studied by a range of spectroscopic techniques. The results indicated that van der Waal interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions were the main driving forces in the binding processes between CA and GQDs. Moreover, the analysis of circular dichroism studies suggested that slight conformational changes occurred after the addition of GQDs. Subsequent catalytic performance change in CA activity induced by GQDs was shown and the activity gradually declined with the increase of GQDs concentrations, resulting in the reduction of biological functions of CA. This type of investigation on nanomaterial-protein interactions is important in biomedical science, which could help to assess the toxic and biological effects of GQDs.

Xiaojuan Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhixiong Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhenzhen Liu: Investigation. Xiqi Ma: Writing – review & editing. Qi Dai: Investigation. Xiaoqiang Wang: Supervision. Baosheng Ge: Funding acquisition. Hua He: Funding acquisition. Fang Huang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Qingdao Municipal People's Livelihood Science and Technology Project (grant no. 17-3-3-76-nsh), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 42061134020, 21874154), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (grant no. ZR2019ZD17), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (18CX02126A), and the Graduate Innovative Engineering Funding project of UPC (YCX2020041).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120369.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
